title: Workbook — The Contemplative Tradition
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in groups. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. What are the three expressions of prayer according to the Catechism? Briefly describe each one.
  2. Why does the Catechism say contemplation is a gift and not a technique? (CCC 2713)
  3. Name and explain the four steps of lectio divina.
  4. Who is called to contemplative prayer? Only monks, or all the baptized? (CCC 2714)
  5. What is the «dark night» according to St. John of the Cross, and how does it differ from a crisis of faith?
  6. In the scene of Martha and Mary (Lk 10:38-42), why does Jesus say Mary «has chosen the good portion»?

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no «right» answers to these personal questions.

1. St. Teresa struggled for eighteen years with prayer before it blossomed. At what stage of your prayer life are you? Do you feel more like Teresa the distracted or Teresa the one in love with God?

Be honest. God doesn't need you to fake fervor. The struggle is normal.

 

 

 

2. Jesus said: «When you pray, go into your room, close the door, and pray to your Father who is in secret» (Mt 6:6). Do you have an «inner room» — a time and place to be alone with God? If not, how could you create one?

It doesn't have to be a chapel. It could be a corner of your room, a park bench, your commute.

 

 

 

3. Do you identify more with Martha (doing, doing, doing) or Mary (sitting and listening)? Do you think your life has a balance between action and contemplation?

Few people are 100% Martha or 100% Mary. Most of us lean more toward one. Which way does your scale tip?

 

 

 

4. Have you ever experienced «dryness» in prayer — feeling nothing, sensing that God is not listening? How did you react?

If you drifted away from prayer because of it, that's okay. Now you know dryness is a normal part of the journey. Could you try again with this new understanding?

 

 

 

5. Charles de Foucauld taught the «practice of the presence of God»: saying from time to time: «God is here, now, with me.» At what moments of your day could you introduce this practice? What would change if you did it for a week?

Be concrete: on waking up, before each meal, leaving home, going to bed. Pick three moments.

 

 

 

Practical Activities

A. Guided Lectio Divina — Psalm 131

This week you will do a complete lectio divina with Psalm 131. Find 15 minutes in a quiet place. Follow these steps and write what you discover:

Step 1 — Lectio (Read): Read Psalm 131 very slowly, three times. Aloud softly or silently. Underline or copy here the word or phrase that strikes you most:

 

 

Step 2 — Meditatio (Meditate): Ask yourself: What does God want to say to me today through this phrase? What aspect of my life does it touch? What does it invite me to change? Repeat the phrase mentally and write your reflections:

 

 

 

Step 3 — Oratio (Pray): Now respond to God in your own words. You can write a brief prayer of thanksgiving, petition, or silence:

 

 

 

Step 4 — Contemplatio (Contemplate): Stay in silence for three minutes. Without writing. Without thinking about the text. Just be with God. If distractions come, let them pass. Afterwards, write one word that sums up what you experienced:

 

B. Practice of the Presence of God

For three days, set silent alarms on your phone three times a day. When they go off, stop for an instant and say inwardly: «God is here, now, with me.» Don't interrupt what you're doing. Just acknowledge. At the end of the third day, write whether you noticed any change (even a small one):

 

 

 

C. The Silence Challenge

Pick one day this week. On that day, every time you feel the impulse to pick up your phone unnecessarily (boredom, habit), don't do it. Instead, close your eyes for ten seconds and breathe slowly. At the end of the day, write how you felt:

 

 

 

Scripture to Memorize

"O LORD, my heart is not lifted up, my eyes are not raised too high; I do not occupy myself with things too great and too marvelous for me. But I have calmed and quieted my soul, like a weaned child with its mother."

— Psalm 131:1-2

"When you pray, go into your room, close the door, and pray to your Father who is in secret. And your Father who sees in secret will reward you."

— Matthew 6:6

Prayer for This Week

Lord, You who let Yourself be found in the gentle whisper,
teach me to listen for You in the silence.
In a world that shouts, give me attentive ears.
In my restless heart, plant peace.
Do not let me be only Martha — running without a soul —,
but give me Mary's courage to sit at Your feet.
May my prayer not be empty words,
but a loving presence.
And may I, in gazing at You, learn to gaze at my brothers and sisters
with the same tenderness with which You gaze at me.
Amen.

Print Workbook
